snippets.title: Built-in: sorted vs list.sort
snippets.code:
xs = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6, 5]

asc = sorted(xs)              # new list, ascending
desc = sorted(xs, reverse=True)

xs.sort()                     # in place; returns None
print(xs)                      # [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 5, 6, 9]
snippets.explanation: `sorted` is non-destructive and works on any iterable; `list.sort` mutates and returns None.

snippets.title: Sorting by a key (and reversing)
snippets.code:
chunks = [
    {'id': 'A', 'score': 0.42, 'cost': 0.001},
    {'id': 'B', 'score': 0.91, 'cost': 0.003},
    {'id': 'C', 'score': 0.55, 'cost': 0.001},
]

by_score_desc = sorted(chunks, key=lambda c: -c['score'])
by_score_then_cost = sorted(chunks, key=lambda c: (-c['score'], c['cost']))

print([c['id'] for c in by_score_desc])         # ['B', 'C', 'A']
print([c['id'] for c in by_score_then_cost])    # ['B', 'C', 'A']
snippets.explanation: Tuples as keys give lexicographic multi-key sort; negate a number to flip its direction.

snippets.title: Multi-pass stable sort
snippets.code:
rows = [
    {'name': 'a', 'score': 90, 'time': 12.0},
    {'name': 'b', 'score': 90, 'time': 10.5},
    {'name': 'c', 'score': 80, 'time': 11.0},
]

# Sort by *secondary* key first, then *primary*:
rows.sort(key=lambda r: r['time'])     # secondary
rows.sort(key=lambda r: -r['score'])    # primary  (stable: keeps secondary order within ties)
print([(r['name'], r['score'], r['time']) for r in rows])
# [('b', 90, 10.5), ('a', 90, 12.0), ('c', 80, 11.0)]
snippets.explanation: Because Timsort is stable, two passes give you a clean multi-key sort without building tuple keys.

snippets.title: Top-k with a heap — avoid the full sort
snippets.code:
import heapq

scores = [(0.42, 'A'), (0.91, 'B'), (0.55, 'C'), (0.88, 'D'), (0.12, 'E')]

top2 = heapq.nlargest(2, scores)            # by full-tuple compare
top2_by_score = heapq.nlargest(2, scores, key=lambda x: x[0])

print(top2)            # [(0.91, 'B'), (0.88, 'D')]
print(top2_by_score)   # [(0.91, 'B'), (0.88, 'D')]
snippets.explanation: O(n log k) instead of O(n log n); especially valuable when k ≪ n.

snippets.title: A tiny mergesort (educational)
snippets.code:
def mergesort(xs):
    if len(xs) <= 1: return xs
    mid = len(xs) // 2
    left, right = mergesort(xs[:mid]), mergesort(xs[mid:])
    out, i, j = [], 0, 0
    while i < len(left) and j < len(right):
        if left[i] <= right[j]:    # `<=` keeps it stable
            out.append(left[i]); i += 1
        else:
            out.append(right[j]); j += 1
    out.extend(left[i:]); out.extend(right[j:])
    return out

print(mergesort([5, 2, 8, 1, 9, 3, 7, 4]))   # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9]
snippets.explanation: For real workloads use Python's built-in Timsort — this is just to make the divide-and-conquer pattern explicit.
